mnemonics.title: HEART Framework
mnemonics.content: Remember HEART: H-Appiness (satisfaction), E-Ngagement (involvement), A-Doption (new users), R-ETention (returning users), T-ask Success (completion)

mnemonics.title: SMART Goals
mnemonics.content: SMART: Specific (clear), Measurable (numbers), Achievable (realistic), Relevant (aligned), Time-bound (deadline)

mnemonics.title: Usability Trinity
mnemonics.content: Usability = Effectiveness + Efficiency + Satisfaction (EES) - the three pillars of usable design

acronyms.expansion: Happiness, Engagement, Adoption, Retention, Task Success
acronyms.usage: Framework for measuring user experience metrics
Criteria for setting effective UX goals
Overall experience a user has when interacting with a product
Standards for making web content accessible to all users
Measurable values that show goal achievement
Metric measuring user loyalty and satisfaction

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a pyramid: Business Goals at the top (why), UX Goals in the middle (what experience), Interface Goals at the bottom (how it looks)
Think of a heart pumping life through the product: Happiness (feeling), Engagement (activity), Adoption (new blood), Retention (keeping blood), Task Success (function)
Think of a user as a worker: Can they do the job? (Effectiveness), How fast? (Efficiency), Do they like it? (Satisfaction)

analogies.analogy: UX Goals are like a bridge connecting two islands - User Needs (one island) and Business Objectives (other island), enabling safe passage between them
Setting UX goals without SMART criteria is like planning a road trip without knowing destination, timeline, or budget - you'll never know if you succeeded
Business goals are the CEO's vision, UX goals are the manager's strategy, Interface goals are the employee's daily tasks - all must align for success
